content.content:
ABSTRACT: Agent Skills are structured packages of procedural knowledge that augment large language model (LLM) agents at inference time. Despite rapid adoption, there is no standard way to measure whether they actually help. We present SkillsBench, a benchmark whose current inventory contains 87 tasks across 8 domains paired with curated Skills and deterministic verifiers. Our latest aggregate evaluation runs the 87-task benchmark under matched no-Skills and curated-Skills conditions for 18 model-harness configurations. Curated Skills raise the average pass rate from 33.9% to 50.5% (+16.6 percentage points; 25.5% normalized gain), with configuration-level gains ranging from +4.1 to +25.7 pp. Focused Skills with at most three modules outperform larger or exhaustive bundles, and smaller models with Skills can match larger models without them. SkillsBench establishes paired evaluation as the foundation for rigorous measurement of Skill efficacy on agentic, expertise-heavy work.

TITLE: SkillsBench: Benchmarking How Well Agent Skills Work Across Diverse Tasks
DATE: 2026/02/13
ALL AUTHORS: ['Li, Xiangyi', 'Liu, Yimin', 'Chen, Wenbo', 'You, Bingran', 'Di, Zonglin', 'He, Yifeng', 'Zheng, Shenghan', 'Choe, Kyoung Whan', 'Sun, Jiankai', 'Wang, Shuyi', 'Tao, Chujun', 'Li, Binxu', 'Zhao, Xuandong', 'Geng, Hejia', 'Wu, Xiaojun', 'Zhou, Junwei', 'Chen, Xiaokun', 'Xing, Hanwen', 'Li, Yubo', 'Zeng, Qunhong', 'Wang, Di', 'Wang, Yuanli', 'Chaim, Roey Ben', 'Jiang, Penghao', 'Shen, Haotian', 'Kong, Luyang', 'Liu, Xinyi', 'Wang, Runhui', 'Liu, Xuanqing', 'Li, Jiachen', 'Lan, Xin', 'Lin, Yueqian', 'Ye, Wengao', 'He, Junwei', 'Li, Songlin', 'Zhang, Yue', 'Gao, Yipeng', 'Li, Yijiang', 'Ma, Ze', 'Jing, Liqiang', 'Wang, Tianyu', 'Li, Kaixin', 'Xue, Yiqi', 'Lyu, Haoran', 'He, Yizhuo', 'Tian, Yuchen', 'Wu, Shutong', 'Wang, Bowei', 'Gao, Yixuan', 'Chen, Bo', 'Liu, Litong', 'Cheng, Sikai', 'Bao, Jiajun', 'Tong, Shuaicheng', 'Xu, Shuwen', 'Zhuo, Terry Yue', 'Ye, Tinghan', 'Qi, Qi', 'Li, Miao', 'Liao, Longtai', 'Tan, Zelin', 'Shi, Chang', 'Tang, Xilin', 'Tankasala, Srinath', 'Yuan, Boqin', 'Qian, Yaoyao', 'Tu, Jianhong', 'Wang, Chenguang', 'Sun, Yizhou', 'Wang, Wei', 'Taylor, Aaron', 'Yang, Ziyue', 'Guan, Changkun', 'Dong, Zhikang', 'Zhang, Xinyu', 'Dillmann, Steven', 'Lee, Han-chung', 'Song, Dawn']

ABSTRACT: Evaluating LLM agent trajectories is fundamentally task-specific: a code-debugging agent should be judged on Correctness and Error Handling, not on Fluency or Safety. Yet the dominant paradigm -- LLM-as-Judge with a fixed rubric -- applies the same static dimensions regardless of task, producing systematic mis-evaluation. We present AdaRubric, a framework that (i) adaptively generates task-specific evaluation rubrics from task descriptions via LLM, (ii) evaluates agent trajectories step-by-step with confidence-weighted, per-dimension scoring, and (iii) produces dense reward signals for preference learning. Three composable filtering strategies, including the novel DimensionAwareFilter that provably prevents dimension-level quality masking, yield high-quality DPO preference pairs. On WebArena, ToolBench, and AgentBench, AdaRubric achieves Pearson r = 0.79 human correlation (+0.15 over the strongest baseline), with strong reliability (Krippendorff's alpha = 0.83). DPO models trained on AdaRubric-generated pairs improve task success by +6.8-8.5% over the best baseline. AdaRubric also generalises zero-shot to unseen domains (SWE-bench) and extends to multimodal agents (VisualWebArena, OSWorld) without modification. Our code is available at: github.com/alphadl/AdaRubrics

TITLE: AdaRubric: Task-Adaptive Rubrics for Reliable LLM Agent Evaluation and Reward Learning
DATE: 2026/03/22
ALL AUTHORS: ['Ding, Liang']

LLM Observability Trace, monitor, and alert on production LLM systems. AI Red Teaming Stress-test LLM apps against adversarial attacks. AI Governance Enforce AI standards and controls across teams. Community Blog Bedtime stories on AI reliability. Knowledge Base Manual to navigate the evals landscape. DeepEval The open-source LLM evaluation framework. DeepTeam The open-source LLM red teaming framework. Docs Pricing Careers 0 Book a Demo Sign Up Products LLM Evaluation LLM Observability AI Red Teaming AI Governance Community Blog Knowledge Base DeepEval DeepTeam Docs Pricing Careers Book a Demo Back Blog LLM Agent Evaluation Metrics in 2026: Tool Calling, Task Completion, Reasoning, and Trace-Based Evals Jun 1, 2026 · 17 min read Kritin Vongthongsri Co-founder @ Confident AI. LLM Evals & Safety Wizard. Previously ML + CS @ Princeton researching self-driving cars. Last weekend, I asked Cursor to ship an internal analytics dashboard. Instead, it spiraled through a series of unfortunate tool calls, planning cycles, and greps. I ended up with an overheated laptop, a 404 page, and a bill that cost an arm and a leg (mind you, this was Opus 4.8 on extra high). An agent that failed to ship the feature after a long and expensive trajectory. That’s the problem with LLM agents in 2026: a small misunderstanding, bad tool call, or broken assumption can compound across hundreds of reasoning loops. An agent can look busy, reason intelligently, call the right-looking tools, and still fail to complete the task. And even when it succeeds, it may have used the wrong tools, passed bad inputs, looped needlessly, or torched all your tokens to get there. LLM agent evaluation is how you prevent those failures before they compound in production. More importantly, it tells you why an agent failed — which tool, input, reasoning step, or handoff actually broke — instead of leaving you to guess. In this guide, I’ll break down the metrics that matter — task completion, step efficiency, argument correctness, tool correctness, plan adherence, plan quality, reasoning quality, answer relevancy, faithfulness, safety, latency, and cost — and when to use each one across end-to-end, trajectory-level, and component-level LLM agent evaluation. (Update: Part 2 on evaluating LLM agents is here!) TL;DR LLM agent evaluation is different from evaluating a plain LLM app. Agents plan, call tools, reason, and hand off work, so the final answer is only the last step of a longer run.

AUTORUBRIC ABSTRACT: Techniques for reliable rubric-based LLM evaluation -- ensemble judging, bias mitigation, few-shot calibration -- are scattered across papers with inconsistent terminology and partial implementations. We introduce Autorubric, an open-source framework that unifies these rubric-based LLM evaluation lessons with opinionated defaults: analytic rubrics with binary, ordinal, and nominal criteria; single-judge and ensemble evaluation; few-shot calibration; bias mitigations; and psychometric reliability metrics. We validate on three benchmarks: RiceChem (college chemistry grading, 80\% accuracy with 5-shot calibration), ResearcherBench (deep research evaluation, 931 criteria, cross-judge agreement analysis), and CHARM-100, a new chatbot evaluation dataset combining all three criterion types with ground truth labels (87\% binary accuracy, moderate-to-substantial $\kappa$). Beyond measurement, per-criterion scores and explanations serve as optimization signals. We demonstrate how Autorubric's rubric-evaluation explanations raise a peer review agent's score from 0.47 to 0.85 (above the 0.82 expert-curated baseline), and its scores serve as RL rewards to produce statistically significant improvement on AdvancedIF (+0.039, Wilcoxon $p = 0.032$) with positive transfer to IFEval. In all of these cases, Autorubric enabled us to rapidly operationalize various rubric design choices and best practices with minimal effort.

TITLE: Autorubric: Unifying Rubric-based LLM Evaluation
DATE: 2026/02/13
ALL AUTHORS: ['Rao, Delip', 'Callison-Burch, Chris']

We read twelve well-known LLM agent benchmark papers and recorded, dimension by dimension, what each paper actually says about how its evaluation was run. The motivation came from a familiar frustration: two papers will report results on the same benchmark with the same model name and disagree, and you cannot tell why -- the scaffold, the sampling settings, the subset, or the evaluator version. In many cases the published artifact does not let you answer. This paper is an implementation report on the attempt. We designed a small audit schema (five fields: benchmark identity, harness specification, inference settings, cost reporting, failure breakdown), wrote a scoring codebook with the boundary cases we hit during pilot scoring, applied it to twelve canonical papers (eight agent, four classical static), and recorded what we saw. We score the disclosure of an agent run, not its correctness, and make no claim that disclosure implies a trustworthy result. The mean audit score across the eight agent-benchmark papers is 0.38 (out of 1.0), and across the four classical static benchmarks 0.66; the largest gap is on cost (none of the eight agent benchmark papers disclose inference cost in any form) and on harness specification (none fully disclose a content-addressed container image of the evaluation environment). We release the schema as a JSON Schema file, the codebook as a Markdown document, and the raw scoring sheet as a CSV. The scoring was performed by a single auditor in one pass; a multi-rater audit is the natural next step, and we discuss what we think it would change.

2026-05-20T17:02:36Z
Pilot audit of 12 LLM agent benchmark papers; schema, codebook, and per-paper scoring sheet released. Submission to IEEE Big Data 2026


Mahdi Naser Moghadasi
BrightMind AI, Texas Tech University

content.text:
Below is a comprehensive, structured report of findings across all areas requested.

---

## Category 1: Agent Skill-Specific Evaluation Tools (purpose-built for SKILL.md / agent skill testing)

### 1.1 Anthropic Skill Creator
- **Source:** https://github.com/anthropics/claude-plugins-official (plugin: `skill-creator`)
- **Language:** Plugin format (skill-based)
- **Stars:** Official Anthropic repository
- **What it is:** Anthropic's official eval infrastructure for skill authoring. Ships three specialized eval agents:
  - **Grader** -- assertion checking + claim extraction
  - **Comparator** -- blind A/B testing of skill vs. no-skill
  - **Analyzer** -- post-hoc root-cause analysis of failures
- **Key features:** Parallel eval execution with isolated contexts, automated description optimization with train/test split (60/40), benchmark tracking (pass rate, tokens, time with variance analysis), HTML eval viewer with qualitative + quantitative tabs.

### 1.2 SkillGrade
- **Source:** https://github.com/mgechev/skillgrade
- **Language:** TypeScript
- **Stars:** 530
- **What it is:** "Unit tests for your agent skills." Tests that AI agents correctly discover and use your skills.
- **Implementation pattern:** YAML-based eval config (`eval.yaml`), runs trials in Docker containers, supports deterministic graders and LLM-rubric graders. Auto-detects agent from API key (Gemini, Claude, Codex, ACP, OpenCode). Generates CLI and browser-based preview reports.
- **Key features:** `--smoke` (5 trials), `--reliable` (15), `--regression` (30) presets; CI mode with pass-rate thresholds; workspace fixture injection into containers.
- **Config example:**
  ```yaml
  version: "1"
  defaults:
    agent: gemini
    provider: docker
    trials: 5
    threshold: 0.8
  tasks:
    - name: fix-linting-errors
      instruction: "Use the superlint tool to fix coding standard violations in app.js."
      graders:
        - type: deterministic
        - type: llm_rubric
  ```

### 1.3 Agent Skills Eval
- **Source:** https://github.com/darkrishabh/agent-skills-eval
- **Language:** TypeScript
- **Stars:** 602
- **What it is:** A test runner for agentskills.io-style AI agent skills. Runs every eval twice -- `with_skill` vs `without_skill` (baseline) -- and uses a judge model to grade both.
- **Implementation pattern:** CLI (`npx agent-skills-eval ./skills`), produces portable JSON/JSONL artifacts plus static HTML reports. Uses OpenAI-compatible chat models as judges. Supports tool-call assertions.
- **One-liner usage:**
  ```bash
  npx agent-skills-eval ./skills --target gpt-4o-mini --judge gpt-4o-mini --baseline --strict
  ```
- **Output structure:**
  ```
  agent-skills-workspace/
  └── iteration-1/
      ├── meta.json
      ├── benchmark.json
      ├── eval-basic/
      │   ├── with_skill/
      │   └── without_skill/
      └── report/index.html
  ```

### 1.4 Skill Conductor
- **Source:** https://github.com/smixs/skill-conductor
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 104
- **What it is:** Architecture-first skill lifecycle for AI agents. Five modes: CREATE, EVAL, EDIT, REVIEW, PACKAGE. Integrates Anthropic's eval engine (grader/comparator/analyzer agents, blind A/B, benchmarks) with architecture patterns, TDD baseline, and 5-axis scoring.
- **Key features:** Five architecture patterns (Sequential, Iterative, Context-Aware, Domain Intelligence, Multi-MCP); degrees of freedom (Low/Medium/High); TDD RED-before-writing (verify agent fails WITHOUT skill first); 5-axis scoring: Discovery, Clarity, Efficiency, Robustness, Completeness (1-10 each, 45-50 = production).
- **Eval infrastructure:**
  ```
                    SKILL
                     |
           +---------+---------+
           |         |         |
        Grader     A/B      Analyzer
       (assertions)(blind   (root cause)
        + claims)  compare)
  ```

### 1.5 OpenJudge
- **Source:** https://github.com/agentscope-ai/OpenJudge
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 679
- **What it is:** A unified evaluation framework for AI applications with ready-to-use graders (50+). Converts grading results into reward signals for fine-tuning.
- **Key features specific to agent skills:** 5 LLM-based graders for evaluating AI Agent Skill packages -- threat analysis (AITech taxonomy), declaration alignment, completeness, relevance, and design quality. Has an online playground at openjudge.me/app.
- **Grader categories:** General (relevance, similarity, syntax check), Agent (tool selection, memory, plan, trajectory), Multimodal.
- **Ecosystem:** PawBench (Model x Harness co-evaluation benchmark -- 150 tasks, 9 models, 3 harnesses).

### 1.6 Eval Layer
- **Source:** https://github.com/erezweinstein5/eval-layer
- **Language:** Claude Code skill (framework-agnostic)
- **Stars:** 11
- **What it is:** A Claude Code skill that adds a rubric-based eval layer to any agent project. Generates rubric, test cases, judge prompt, and harness. Returns a weighted score plus a judge-leniency signal.
- **Implementation pattern:** 7-field metadata contract for cross-framework comparison (recommendation, latency_ms, tool_calls, input_tokens, output_tokens, model_id, error). Judge calibration via leniency metric.

---

## Category 2: General Agent Evaluation Frameworks

### 2.1 RagaAI Catalyst
- **Source:** https://github.com/raga-ai-hub/RagaAI-Catalyst
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 16,150
- **What it is:** Python SDK for Agent AI Observability, Monitoring and Evaluation. Includes agent/LLM/tools tracing, debugging multi-agentic systems, self-hosted dashboard with timeline and execution graph views.
- **Features:** Dataset management, evaluation management (20+ metrics including Faithfulness, Hallucination, Context Relevancy), trace management, synthetic data generation, guardrail management, red-teaming.

### 2.2 ASSERT
- **Source:** https://github.com/responsibleai/ASSERT
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 158
- **What it is:** Requirement-driven evaluation harness for AI agents. Generates behavior-specific test cases from natural language specifications, runs them against any target (hosted models, callable wrappers, OTel-traced agents), and produces local-first artifacts.
- **Key features:** Spec-driven coverage (test cases generated from product requirements); integrations with LiteLLM (100+ model endpoints) and OpenInference (LangGraph, CrewAI, OpenAI Agents SDK, DSPy, LlamaIndex, AutoGen); agent trace-grounded judgment via OpenTelemetry spans; bundled local viewer.

### 2.3 IntellAgent
- **Source:** https://github.com/plurai-ai/intellagent
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 1,237
- **What it is:** A multi-agent framework for comprehensive diagnosis and optimization of conversational agents. Simulates thousands of realistic edge-case interactions to uncover hidden failure points.
- **How it works:** (1) Decomposes prompt into a policy graph, (2) Samples policies and generates scenarios, (3) Simulates user-chatbot interaction, (4) Critiques conversation and provides feedback on tested policies.
- **Key features:** Policy graph decomposition, edge-case scenario generation, comprehensive performance evaluations. Approx $0.10 cost per sample.

### 2.4 DeepEval
- **Source:** https://github.com/confident-ai/deepeval
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 16,421
- **What it is:** The LLM Evaluation Framework. Pytest-integrated evaluation for LLM applications. Provides evaluation metrics, unit-test-style testing, and CI integration.

---

## Category 3: Observability & Evaluation Platforms

### 3.1 Langfuse
- **Source:** https://github.com/langfuse/langfuse
- **Language:** TypeScript
- **Stars:** 29,594
- **What it is:** Open source AI engineering platform: LLM evals, observability, metrics, prompt management, playground, datasets. Integrates with OpenTelemetry, LangChain, OpenAI SDK, LiteLLM.

### 3.2 LangWatch
- **Source:** https://github.com/langwatch/langwatch
- **Language:** TypeScript
- **Stars:** 3,312
- **What it is:** Platform for LLM evaluations and AI agent testing. Provides datasets, evaluation workflows, observability, and DSPy integration.

### 3.3 OpenLIT
- **Source:** https://github.com/openlit/openlit
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 2,543
- **What it is:** Open source platform for AI Engineering: OpenTelemetry-native LLM Observability, GPU Monitoring, Guardrails, Evaluations, Prompt Management. Integrates with 50+ LLM Providers, VectorDBs, Agent Frameworks and GPUs.

### 3.4 Future AGI
- **Source:** https://github.com/future-agi/future-agi
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 1,218
- **What it is:** Open-source, end-to-end platform for evaluating, observing, and improving LLM and AI agent applications. Tracing, Evals, Simulations, Datasets, Gateway, Guardrails. Self-hostable (Apache 2.0).

---

## Category 4: Agent Testing Harnesses & Benchmarks

### 4.1 Harbor
- **Source:** https://github.com/harbor-framework/harbor
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 2,652
- **What it is:** Framework for running agent evaluations and creating/using RL environments. Includes terminal-based benchmarks and RL training environments.

### 4.2 AWS Agent Evaluation
- **Source:** https://github.com/awslabs/agent-evaluation
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 368
- **What it is:** A generative AI-powered framework for testing virtual agents (AWS domain -- Lex, Connect, etc.).

### 4.3 Bananalyzer
- **Source:** https://github.com/reworkd/bananalyzer
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 327
- **What it is:** Open source AI Agent evaluation framework for web tasks. Tests agents on web navigation and form-filling tasks.

### 4.4 ServiceNow AgentLab
- **Source:** https://github.com/ServiceNow/AgentLab
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 591
- **What it is:** Framework for developing, testing, and benchmarking web agents on diverse tasks (WebArena, WorkArena, VisualWebArena, WebLinx). Supports large-scale parallel experiments via Ray.

### 4.5 Mozilla Any Agent
- **Source:** https://github.com/mozilla-ai/any-agent
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 1,177
- **What it is:** A single interface to use and evaluate different agent frameworks. Supports A2A and MCP protocols.

### 4.6 JFrog Agent Belt
- **Source:** https://github.com/jfrog/agent-belt
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 16
- **What it is:** Reproducible evaluation for AI coding agents. Multi-turn scenarios against Claude Code, Codex, Copilot, Cursor, Gemini CLI, Goose, OpenCode. Uses rule checks, workspace diffs, and multi-judge LLM consensus. Pass^k variance across trials. Git worktrees with optional Docker sandbox.

### 4.7 CyberGym
- **Source:** https://github.com/sunblaze-ucb/cybergym
- **Language:** Python
- **Stars:** 437
- **What it is:** Large-scale cybersecurity evaluation framework for assessing AI agents on real-world vulnerability analysis tasks.

---

## Category 5: Emerging / Niche Tools

| Tool | Stars | Description |
|------|-------|-------------|
| **agents-md-evals** | 3 | Evaluate/optimize AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md through A/B testing. Discovers which rules change behavior vs. waste tokens. Built on Anthropic's skill-creator eval framework. |
| **skilljack-evals** | 4 | CLI for evaluating Agent Skills support using multiple models and SDKs. Tests discoverability, instruction adherence, output quality. Runs standalone or as GitHub Action. |
| **eval-coach** | 4 | Agent Skill for Evaluation-Driven Development (EDD) -- 50-40-10 rule for AI evaluation strategies. |
| **agentunit** | 7 | Pytest-inspired evaluation harness for autonomous agents and RAG workflows. Heuristic and LLM-backed metrics. |
| **Themis** | 3 | Intent-first unit testing framework for AI agents in Node.js/TypeScript. |
| **AgentUnit** | 7 | Pytest-inspired evaluation harness for autonomous agents and RAG workflows. |

---

## Category 6: Patterns and Implementation Approaches

**Across all tools found, several distinct evaluation patterns emerge:**

1. **With/Without Baseline Comparison** -- Used by SkillGrade, agent-skills-eval, and Skill Conductor. Run the agent against the same test set twice (with skill loaded, without skill loaded) and measure the delta. This answers the fundamental question: "Does this skill actually change behavior?"

2. **Three-Tier Judging Architecture** -- Anthropic's Grader (assertions/claims) + Comparator (blind A/B) + Analyzer (root cause). Adopted by Skill Conductor and others.

3. **LLM-as-Judge with Calibration** -- Used by Eval Layer and ASSERT. Include human-graded reference cases in the judge prompt, then compute a leniency metric to detect judge drift.

4. **Behavior-Spec-Driven Test Generation** -- ASSERT leads here: write natural-language specifications of expected behaviors, and the framework generates both test cases and judge rubrics automatically.

5. **YAML-Configurable Eval Suites** -- SkillGrade and OpenJudge use declarative YAML configs defining tasks, graders, environments, and thresholds. Enables CI integration with pass/fail gates.

6. **Multi-Axis Scoring** -- Skill Conductor (5-axis: Discovery, Clarity, Efficiency, Robustness, Completeness) and OpenJudge (50+ graders across General/Agent/Multimodal categories) move beyond single-score metrics to multi-dimensional quality assessment.

7. **Observability-Integrated Evaluation** -- RagaAI Catalyst, Langfuse, LangWatch, and OpenLIT all combine tracing/telemetry with evaluation, allowing evaluators to examine tool calls, token usage, latency, and agent trajectories alongside quality scores.

---

|------|-----|-----------------|

| SkillGrade | https://github.com/mgechev/skillgrade | Unit tests for agent skills |
| Agent Skills Eval | https://github.com/darkrishabh/agent-skills-eval | With/without skill comparison |
| Skill Conductor | https://github.com/smixs/skill-conductor | Full skill lifecycle + eval |
| OpenJudge | https://github.com/agentscope-ai/OpenJudge | 50+ graders, skill graders |
| ASSERT | https://github.com/responsibleai/ASSERT | Spec-driven evaluation harness |
| IntellAgent | https://github.com/plurai-ai/intellagent | Simulated scenario testing |
| RagaAI Catalyst | https://github.com/raga-ai-hub/RagaAI-Catalyst | Observability + evaluation |
| DeepEval | https://github.com/confident-ai/deepeval | Pytest-integrated LLM evals |
| Langfuse | https://github.com/langfuse/langfuse | Observability + evals platform |
| LangWatch | https://github.com/langwatch/langwatch | LLM evals + agent testing |
| Eval Layer | https://github.com/erezweinstein5/eval-layer | Rubric-based eval skill |
| Harbor | https://github.com/harbor-framework/harbor | Agent eval + RL environments |
| Agent Belt | https://github.com/jfrog/agent-belt | Reproducible coding agent eval |

content.content:
ABSTRACT: AI agents are an exciting new research direction, and agent development is driven by benchmarks. Our analysis of current agent benchmarks and evaluation practices reveals several shortcomings that hinder their usefulness in real-world applications. First, there is a narrow focus on accuracy without attention to other metrics. As a result, SOTA agents are needlessly complex and costly, and the community has reached mistaken conclusions about the sources of accuracy gains. Our focus on cost in addition to accuracy motivates the new goal of jointly optimizing the two metrics. We design and implement one such optimization, showing its potential to greatly reduce cost while maintaining accuracy. Second, the benchmarking needs of model and downstream developers have been conflated, making it hard to identify which agent would be best suited for a particular application. Third, many agent benchmarks have inadequate holdout sets, and sometimes none at all. This has led to agents that are fragile because they take shortcuts and overfit to the benchmark in various ways. We prescribe a principled framework for avoiding overfitting. Finally, there is a lack of standardization in evaluation practices, leading to a pervasive lack of reproducibility. We hope that the steps we introduce for addressing these shortcomings will spur the development of agents that are useful in the real world and not just accurate on benchmarks.

TITLE: AI Agents That Matter
DATE: 2024/07/01
ALL AUTHORS: ['Kapoor, Sayash', 'Stroebl, Benedikt', 'Siegel, Zachary S.', 'Nadgir, Nitya', 'Narayanan, Arvind']

ABSTRACT: Recent advances in agentic AI have shifted the focus from standalone Large Language Models (LLMs) to integrated systems that combine LLMs with tools, memory, and other agents to perform complex tasks. These multi-agent architectures enable coordinated reasoning, planning, and execution across diverse domains, allowing agents to collaboratively automate complex workflows. Despite these advances, evaluation and assessment of LLM agents and the multi-agent systems they constitute remain a fundamental challenge. Although various approaches have been proposed in the software engineering literature for evaluating conventional software components, existing methods for AI-based systems often overlook the non-deterministic nature of models. This non-determinism introduces behavioral uncertainty during execution, yet existing evaluations rely on binary task completion metrics that fail to capture it. Evaluating agentic systems therefore requires examining additional dimensions, including the agent ability to invoke tools, ingest and retrieve memory, collaborate with other agents, and interact effectively with its environment. These challenges emerged during our ongoing industry collaboration with MontyCloud Inc., when we deployed an agentic system in production. These limitations surfaced during deployment, highlighting practical gaps in the current evaluation methods and the need for a systematic assessment of agent behavior beyond task outcomes. Informed by these observations and established definitions of agentic systems, we propose an end-to-end Agent Assessment Framework with four evaluation pillars encompassing LLMs, Memory, Tools, and Environment. We validate the framework on a representative Autonomous CloudOps use case, where experiments reveal behavioral deviations overlooked by conventional metrics, demonstrating its effectiveness in capturing runtime uncertainties.

TITLE: Beyond Task Completion: An Assessment Framework for Evaluating Agentic AI Systems
DATE: 2025/12/14
ALL AUTHORS: ['Akshathala, Sreemaee', 'Adnan, Bassam', 'Ramesh, Mahisha', 'Vaidhyanathan, Karthik', 'Muhammed, Basil', 'Parthasarathy, Kannan']

content.content:
ABSTRACT: Current agentic AI benchmarks predominantly evaluate task completion accuracy, while overlooking critical enterprise requirements such as cost-efficiency, reliability, and operational stability. Through systematic analysis of 12 main benchmarks and empirical evaluation of state-of-the-art agents, we identify three fundamental limitations: (1) absence of cost-controlled evaluation leading to 50x cost variations for similar precision, (2) inadequate reliability assessment where agent performance drops from 60\% (single run) to 25\% (8-run consistency), and (3) missing multidimensional metrics for security, latency, and policy compliance. We propose \textbf{CLEAR} (Cost, Latency, Efficacy, Assurance, Reliability), a holistic evaluation framework specifically designed for enterprise deployment. Evaluation of six leading agents on 300 enterprise tasks demonstrates that optimizing for accuracy alone yields agents 4.4-10.8x more expensive than cost-aware alternatives with comparable performance. Expert evaluation (N=15) confirms that CLEAR better predicts production success (correlation $\rho=0.83$) compared to accuracy-only evaluation ($\rho=0.41$).

TITLE: Beyond Accuracy: A Multi-Dimensional Framework for Evaluating Enterprise Agentic AI Systems
DATE: 2025/11/18
ALL AUTHORS: ['Mehta, Sushant']

Key sources discovered:

**Academic Survey Papers:**
1. "Evaluation and Benchmarking of LLM Agents: A Survey" - Mohammadi et al. (2025), KDD 2025, arXiv:2507.21504 - Two-dimensional taxonomy: evaluation objectives (behavior, capabilities, reliability, safety) x evaluation process (interaction modes, datasets, metrics, tooling)

**Skill Evaluation-Specific:**
4. "SkillsBench: Benchmarking How Well Agent Skills Work Across Diverse Tasks" - Li et al. (2026), arXiv:2602.12670 - 87 tasks across 8 domains, paired evaluation (no-skill vs curated-skill), curated skills raised pass rate from 33.9% to 50.5%
5. AutoRubric by Delip Rao & Chris Callison-Burch (2026), arXiv:2603.00077 - Open-source framework for rubric-based LLM evaluation with ensemble judging, bias mitigation, few-shot calibration
6. AutoRubric Cookbook: Evaluating Agent Skills - Three-condition comparison (no skill / poor skill / good skill), dimension analysis, failure mode analysis

**Agent Evaluation Frameworks:**
7. AdaRubric - Ding (2026), arXiv:2603.21362 - Task-adaptive rubrics for LLM agent evaluation, Pearson r=0.79 human correlation
8. Agent-as-a-Judge - Zhuge et al. (2025), ICML 2025 - Agentic systems evaluating agentic systems, dramatically outperforms LLM-as-a-Judge
9. CLEAR (Cost, Latency, Efficacy, Assurance, Reliability) - Mehta (2025), arXiv:2511.14136 - Multi-dimensional framework for enterprise agent evaluation

**Key Benchmarks:**
- GAIA (General AI Assistants Benchmark) - 466 real-world questions, multi-modal reasoning, tool use
- AgentBench - 8 environments, 29 LLMs evaluated
- SWE-bench / SWE-bench Verified - Real GitHub issues resolution
- BFCL (Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard) - Tool/function calling accuracy
- WebArena - Web-based agent tasks

**Industry Evaluation Platforms/Guides:**
- Galileo AI - 3-tier rubrics (7 dimensions -> 25 sub-dimensions -> 130 items), LLM-as-judge targeting 0.80+ Spearman
- Langfuse - Three evaluation strategies: Black-box (final response), Glass-box (trajectory), White-box (single step)
- Confident AI / DeepEval - 12 metrics: task completion, step efficiency, argument correctness, tool correctness, plan adherence, plan quality, reasoning quality, answer relevancy, faithfulness, safety, latency, cost
- PEARL - Rubric-driven multi-metric framework

**Best Practices Identified:**
1. Distinguish trajectory metrics (process) from outcome metrics (results)
2. Use controlled comparisons (with-skill vs without-skill)
3. Build hierarchical rubrics (dimensions -> sub-dimensions -> items)
4. Implement LLM-as-Judge with 0.80+ Spearman correlation target
5. Include "cannot assess" option to avoid forced binary verdicts
6. Start with tracing, not scoring
7. Define success criteria before writing evaluators
8. Integrate evaluation into CI/CD
9. Use paired evaluation for skill efficacy measurement
10. Include cost and efficiency metrics alongside accuracy
